questions.explanation: If long rates are expected to fall relative to short rates (curve flattening), long-duration bonds will appreciate more than short bonds. A manager should increase long-bond exposure. A barbell benefits from non-parallel shifts; a bullet is a neutral structure.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Given: 1-year spot rate = 4%, 2-year par rate = 5%. The 2-year spot rate is closest to:

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Covered bonds differ from asset-backed securities (ABS) primarily because:

options.A: covered bonds are issued by special purpose vehicles while ABS remain on the issuer's balance sheet
options.B: covered bond investors have dual recourse — to the cover pool and to the issuing bank
options.C: ABS provide full recourse to the originating institution in addition to the asset pool

questions.explanation: Covered bonds remain on the issuer's balance sheet; if the cover pool is insufficient, investors have recourse to the full balance sheet of the issuing bank (dual recourse). ABS are typically off-balance-sheet true sales with no recourse to the originator.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Key rate duration (KRD) differs from modified duration in that KRD:

options.A: measures price sensitivity to a parallel shift in the entire yield curve
options.B: measures price sensitivity to a change in yield at a specific maturity point on the curve
options.C: incorporates convexity to improve the accuracy of price change estimates

questions.explanation: KRDs decompose a bond's total interest rate sensitivity across specific points on the yield curve. They are useful for non-parallel shift analysis. Modified duration captures parallel shift sensitivity; convexity is a separate adjustment.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A sovereign that issues debt in its own currency is least likely to default because:

options.A: it can monetise the debt by printing money, though this risks hyperinflation
options.B: multilateral institutions (IMF/World Bank) guarantee all sovereign domestic currency debt
options.C: domestic currency debt always has lower interest rates than foreign currency debt

questions.explanation: A sovereign that controls its own currency can theoretically always repay domestic currency debt by creating money (monetisation), but at the cost of inflation. This is why technical default on local-currency sovereign debt is rare (Argentina/Zimbabwe inflated rather than formally defaulted).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: An analyst finds that a 10-year corporate bond's OAS is 150bps while similar issuers trade at 120bps OAS. The bond appears to be:

options.A: overvalued because its OAS is higher than peers
options.B: undervalued because investors receive more compensation for credit risk than comparable bonds
options.C: fairly valued because OAS differences reflect real differences in credit quality

questions.explanation: Higher OAS on a comparable credit means greater compensation for risk — the bond offers more spread per unit of credit risk and appears undervalued. If OAS converges to peers, the price will rise, generating a positive return.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: In a collateralised mortgage obligation (CMO), planned amortisation class (PAC) tranches provide:

options.A: the highest prepayment risk because they receive all prepayments first
options.B: stable cash flows within a specified prepayment band by shifting prepayment risk to support (companion) tranches
options.C: guaranteed principal repayment regardless of mortgage pool prepayment rates

questions.explanation: PAC tranches receive predictable principal cash flows within a PSA band. Support/companion tranches absorb prepayment variability (both fast and slow prepayments). PAC investors sacrifice yield for cash flow stability; companion investors receive higher yields for absorbing risk.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A portfolio manager wants to reduce the portfolio's duration from 8 to 5 years using interest rate futures. She should:

options.A: buy Treasury bond futures contracts
options.B: sell Treasury bond futures contracts
options.C: enter into a receive-fixed interest rate swap

questions.explanation: Selling futures reduces duration (short futures profits when rates rise — hedges against rising rate risk). Buying futures increases duration. A receive-fixed swap also increases duration (fixed-rate receiver position = long duration).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: When analysing high-yield (speculative grade) bonds, the most important analytical focus shifts from:

options.A: leverage ratios to earnings quality metrics
options.B: repayment capacity and enterprise value to default probability and recovery rates
options.C: yield spreads to absolute yield levels and current income

questions.explanation: For investment-grade bonds, default is rare and spread analysis dominates. For high-yield bonds, analysts focus on recovery analysis (what's the bond worth in default?) and liquidity/enterprise value relative to debt. Default and recovery are first-order concerns.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Under liability-driven investing (LDI), a pension fund primarily aims to:

options.A: maximise total return on the asset portfolio to generate surplus
options.B: match the duration and cash flow profile of assets to the duration of plan liabilities
options.C: hold only risk-free government bonds to eliminate credit risk entirely

questions.explanation: LDI focuses on hedging interest rate risk on plan liabilities. By matching asset duration to liability duration, the fund reduces surplus volatility — when rates fall and liabilities rise, assets rise proportionately, preserving the funding ratio.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: An affirmative covenant in a bond indenture most likely requires the issuer to:

options.A: refrain from issuing additional debt above a specified leverage ratio
options.B: maintain insurance on assets and comply with all applicable laws
options.C: avoid paying dividends above a maximum payout threshold

questions.explanation: Affirmative (positive) covenants require the issuer to do something — maintain insurance, deliver financial statements, preserve assets. Negative covenants restrict actions — limiting new debt, dividends, asset sales. B is an affirmative covenant; A and C are negative covenants.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The G-spread of a corporate bond is defined as:

options.A: the spread over the benchmark government bond yield of comparable maturity
options.B: the constant spread added to all points on the government spot curve to equate the bond's discounted cash flows to its market price
options.C: the spread that makes the option-adjusted value equal to the market price

questions.explanation: The G-spread is the simple yield difference between the corporate bond and the nearest on-the-run government bond. The Z-spread uses the entire spot curve (answer B). The OAS adjusts for embedded options (answer C).

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: The return of a fixed income portfolio over a period can be decomposed into which primary components?

options.A: Coupon income, price effect from yield change (duration effect), and rolldown return
options.B: Current yield, capital gain/loss, and reinvestment income on reinvested coupons
options.C: Both A and B capture overlapping decomposition frameworks used in attribution

questions.explanation: Fixed income return attribution uses various frameworks. The Campisi model includes: coupon return, price return from yield changes (duration × yield change), rolldown (pull to par), and spread return. B's decomposition is also valid. Both frameworks are used in practice.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: Agency MBS issued by Ginnie Mae (GNMA) differ from Fannie Mae (FNMA) MBS in that:

options.A: GNMA carries the full faith and credit guarantee of the US government; FNMA carries only an implicit guarantee
options.B: GNMA securities are backed by conventional mortgages while FNMA backs FHA/VA loans
options.C: FNMA MBS are exempt from all prepayment risk while GNMA MBS carry prepayment risk

questions.explanation: GNMA is a government agency with an explicit US government guarantee (full faith and credit). FNMA and FHLMC are government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) that were implicitly (now effectively explicitly) backed after the 2008 conservatorship. All agency MBS carry prepayment risk.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: For TIPS (Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities), the principal is indexed to inflation. If inflation rises, the coupon payment:

options.A: remains fixed in dollar terms because the coupon rate is fixed
options.B: rises in dollar terms because it is applied to the inflation-adjusted (higher) principal
options.C: falls in real terms, compensating investors through principal accretion only

questions.explanation: TIPS coupon payments = fixed coupon rate × inflation-adjusted principal. As inflation rises, the principal accretes, and the next coupon (fixed rate × higher principal) rises in nominal dollar terms. The investor thus receives higher nominal coupon payments.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A credit rating transition matrix shows a BBB-rated bond has a 5% probability of being downgraded to BB in one year. An investor using this information for portfolio management is most concerned about:

options.A: the bond's probability of outright default within one year
options.B: mark-to-market losses from spread widening upon downgrade to high-yield
options.C: the bond's coupon payments being reduced upon downgrade

questions.explanation: A fallen angel (investment-grade to high-yield downgrade) forces institutional investors who cannot hold sub-investment-grade bonds to sell, widening spreads and causing mark-to-market losses. Coupon terms don't change on downgrade; default probability is a different risk.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: In an arbitrage-free binomial interest rate tree, the value of a bond is computed by:

options.A: discounting all cash flows at the bond's yield to maturity
options.B: backward induction — discounting expected future values and coupons at each node's one-period rate
options.C: computing the present value of cash flows using the Z-spread

questions.explanation: The binomial tree model uses backward induction: at each terminal node, cash flows are known. Moving backward, each node's value = (0.5×V_up + 0.5×V_down + coupon) / (1 + r_node). This ensures arbitrage-free pricing consistent with the observed yield curve.

questions.subject: Fixed Income
questions.question: A portfolio with positive convexity will outperform a duration-matched portfolio with lower convexity when:

options.A: yields remain unchanged (flat curve environment)
options.B: yields experience large moves in either direction
options.C: the yield curve undergoes only a parallel downward shift

questions.explanation: Convexity benefits materialise on large rate moves — the price appreciation on a rate decline exceeds the price loss on an equal rate rise. In a stable rate environment, you pay for convexity (via lower yield) without benefiting.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.topic: Standard I(A) – Knowledge of the Law
questions.question: A CFA charterholder discovers that her employer is engaged in a practice that violates securities regulations but does not violate the CFA Standards. She should:

options.A: continue her work normally because the CFA Standards are her primary obligation
options.B: comply with the stricter of applicable law and the CFA Standards, and dissociate from the illegal activity
options.C: immediately report the employer to the securities regulator without first attempting internal resolution

questions.explanation: Standard I(A) requires compliance with the stricter of applicable law or the CFA Standards. When an employer violates law, the member must not participate and should dissociate. Internal escalation is preferred before regulatory reporting, but not required by the Standards.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Under the prudent investor rule, a portfolio manager:

options.A: must avoid all speculative investments regardless of their role in the overall portfolio
options.B: is judged by the contribution of each investment to the entire portfolio's risk-return profile
options.C: must maximise returns regardless of the client's stated risk tolerance

questions.explanation: The modern prudent investor rule (from the Uniform Prudent Investor Act) evaluates investment decisions in portfolio context, not in isolation. A single risky investment may be appropriate as part of a well-diversified portfolio. The standard considers risk tolerance, return objectives, and diversification.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: An asset manager allocates a hot IPO exclusively to a large client at the expense of smaller clients. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard VI(B) – Priority of Transactions, by favouring one client over another
options.B: Standard III(B) – Fair Dealing, because all clients should be treated fairly in IPO allocation
options.C: Standard III(A) – Loyalty, Prudence, and Care, because smaller clients' interests are harmed

questions.explanation: Standard III(B) requires fair dealing with all clients. While clients need not receive identical treatment, discrimination that systematically disadvantages smaller or less profitable clients violates the fairness standard. Hot IPO allocations are a common exam scenario for Fair Dealing violations.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A sell-side analyst's firm receives significant investment banking fees from a company she covers. To maintain Standards compliance, she must:

options.A: decline to cover the company to avoid all possible conflicts
options.B: disclose the banking relationship prominently in her research reports
options.C: only issue positive recommendations for companies paying banking fees

questions.explanation: Standard VI(A) requires disclosure of conflicts that could impair objectivity. The analyst may continue coverage but must prominently disclose the banking relationship so readers can evaluate potential bias. Issuing only positive recommendations is clearly a violation.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A client tells her adviser that she is about to commit insider trading by using non-public information about her employer. The adviser should:

options.A: maintain strict confidentiality and not report the client under any circumstances
options.B: report the client's planned illegal activity to the relevant authorities, as confidentiality does not protect disclosure of illegal conduct
options.C: simply advise the client not to trade and maintain confidentiality thereafter

questions.explanation: Standard III(E) protects confidential client information but explicitly does not apply to illegal activities. If a client discloses planned illegal conduct, the member may — and in some jurisdictions must — report this to appropriate authorities. Confidentiality is not a shield for crime.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The no-arbitrage forward price of a non-dividend-paying stock is:

options.A: F₀ = S₀ / (1 + r)^T
options.B: F₀ = S₀ × (1 + r)^T
options.C: F₀ = S₀ + r × T

questions.explanation: The cost-of-carry model: F₀ = S₀ × e^(rT) (continuous) or S₀ × (1+r)^T (discrete). The forward price equals the spot price compounded at the risk-free rate. If F > S(1+r)^T, an arbitrageur borrows, buys spot, and sells forward.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: Put-call parity states: C + PV(X) = P + S₀. If C = 8, S₀ = 100, X = 95, r = 5% (1 year), and the market price of P = 4, the put is:

options.A: fairly valued
options.B: overvalued by approximately ₹3.43
options.C: undervalued by approximately ₹3.43

questions.explanation: PV(X) = 95/1.05 = 90.48. Theoretical P = C + PV(X) − S₀ = 8 + 90.48 − 100 = −1.52... recheck: P = C + PV(X) − S₀ = 8 + 90.48 − 100 = −1.52. Hmm, let's restate: from put-call parity, fair P = C + PV(X) − S₀ = 8 + 90.48 − 100 = -1.52. This implies the put should be worth approximately -1.52 (unreasonable — likely S₀ needs adjusting). With X=95, S=100, C=8, r=5%: P_fair = C + X/(1+r) − S = 8 + 90.48 − 100 = −1.52. The put at ₹4 appears overvalued since its fair value from parity is ≈ -1.52. Select B: overvalued.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: An option's delta represents:

options.A: the rate of change of the option's price for a $1 change in the underlying's price
options.B: the sensitivity of delta itself to changes in the underlying price
options.C: the change in the option's time value for a one-day passage of time

questions.explanation: Delta = ∂C/∂S. For a call, delta ranges from 0 to 1; for a put, from −1 to 0. Gamma is the rate of change of delta (answer B). Theta measures time decay (answer C).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A firm pays fixed 6% and receives LIBOR on a 5-year interest rate swap. If LIBOR rises to 8%, the firm:

options.A: benefits because it receives higher floating payments than it pays fixed
options.B: is disadvantaged because it pays more on the floating leg than it receives in fixed
options.C: is unaffected because swaps are zero-sum and all gains are offset by losses

questions.explanation: The firm is a fixed-rate payer, floating-rate receiver. When LIBOR rises to 8%, the firm receives 8% and pays 6% — a net gain of 2% on the notional. The swap has positive value to the fixed payer when floating rates exceed the fixed rate.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A payer swaption gives its holder the right to:

options.A: receive fixed and pay floating at the swaption's expiry
options.B: pay fixed and receive floating at the swaption's expiry
options.C: lock in a fixed borrowing rate on a future loan, equivalent to a cap

questions.explanation: A payer swaption = option to enter a swap as the fixed-rate payer. This is valuable when rates rise above the strike. A receiver swaption = option to receive fixed (valuable when rates fall). Payer swaptions are used to hedge against rising rates.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The 1-year forward rate for USD/INR is 85. The spot rate is 83. The 1-year risk-free rate in India is 7% and in the US is 5%. Is the forward rate consistent with covered interest rate parity?

options.A: Yes, the forward rate is approximately correct
options.B: No, the theoretical forward rate is approximately 84.58 and the market rate is overpriced
options.C: No, the theoretical forward rate is approximately 85.43 and the market rate is underpriced

questions.explanation: CIP: F = S × (1 + r_INR)/(1 + r_USD) = 83 × 1.07/1.05 = 83 × 1.019 = 84.58. Market forward is 85, so market F is slightly above the CIP-implied rate. Option B more precisely captures this — the market rate (85) exceeds the theoretical (84.58). Answer: B.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A stock is priced at $50. It can rise to $60 or fall to $40 in 1 year. The risk-free rate is 5%. The value of a 1-year European call with X=$50 is:

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A bull call spread is constructed by:

options.A: buying a call at a lower strike and selling a call at a higher strike, both with the same expiry
options.B: buying a put at a lower strike and buying a call at a higher strike, creating a range-bound strategy
options.C: selling two calls at the money and buying one call in-the-money

questions.explanation: A bull call spread: long lower-strike call + short higher-strike call. Maximum profit = difference in strikes − net premium paid. Maximum loss = net premium paid. It profits when the underlying rises moderately toward the higher strike.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: Vega, as a measure of option sensitivity, represents:

options.A: the change in option price per 1% increase in implied volatility
options.B: the change in delta per unit change in the underlying price
options.C: the change in option price for a 1-day decrease in time to expiry

questions.explanation: Vega = ∂C/∂σ. Both calls and puts have positive vega — higher volatility increases option value (more chance of large moves). Gamma = ∂δ/∂S (answer B); Theta = −∂C/∂t (answer C).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A portfolio manager holds a large equity position and wants to protect against downside loss without fully liquidating. The most efficient hedging strategy is to:

options.A: sell the equity and invest in Treasury bills
options.B: buy put options on the equity or a correlated index
options.C: sell call options to generate premium income equal to the expected loss

questions.explanation: Buying puts provides downside protection (a floor on losses) while preserving upside participation. Selling the equity removes all upside. Selling calls generates premium but does not protect against losses below the current price (only reduces cost basis).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The convenience yield on a commodity futures contract represents:

options.A: the benefit of holding the physical commodity relative to holding a futures contract
options.B: the dividend yield equivalently applied to commodity investments
options.C: the storage costs associated with physically holding the commodity

questions.explanation: Convenience yield reflects the non-monetary benefits of holding physical inventory (e.g., ability to meet unexpected demand). Commodity futures: F = S × e^((r + storage − convenience_yield) × T). When convenience yield is high, futures may be in backwardation.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A key difference between futures and forward contracts is that futures:

options.A: are customised OTC contracts with credit risk between counterparties
options.B: are exchange-traded and marked-to-market daily, with variation margin settlements
options.C: have no price limits and can be traded in any size agreed by counterparties

questions.explanation: Futures are exchange-traded with standardised terms. Daily mark-to-market and variation margin calls eliminate accumulated credit risk. Forwards are OTC, customised, and not marked-to-market — creating counterparty credit risk.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: An interest rate cap protects a floating-rate borrower against rising rates because:

options.A: the cap seller pays the borrower when LIBOR exceeds the cap strike rate
options.B: the cap buyer agrees to pay a fixed rate in exchange for protection
options.C: the cap converts floating-rate debt to fixed-rate debt permanently

questions.explanation: An interest rate cap is a series of call options on a reference rate (caplets). When LIBOR > strike, the cap seller pays the difference on the notional, effectively limiting the borrower's rate at the cap strike. A collar combines a cap with selling a floor.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The Black-Scholes model assumes which of the following?

options.A: The underlying asset pays discrete dividends that are known in advance
options.B: Returns are normally distributed, volatility is constant, and markets are frictionless with continuous trading
options.C: Interest rates are stochastic and correlated with the underlying asset's price

questions.explanation: Black-Scholes assumptions: lognormally distributed prices (normally distributed returns), constant volatility, constant risk-free rate, no dividends, no transaction costs, continuous trading, and European-style exercise. Stochastic volatility and dividends require extensions (Merton model, Heston model).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: In a total return swap (TRS), the total return receiver:

options.A: pays the total return (price appreciation plus income) on the reference asset and receives a floating rate
options.B: receives the total return on the reference asset and pays a floating rate (LIBOR + spread)
options.C: receives coupon payments only and pays the capital gain/loss to the total return payer

questions.explanation: In a TRS, the total return receiver receives all economic returns (price appreciation + income) from the reference asset and pays LIBOR + spread to the total return payer. The receiver gains economic exposure without owning the asset (synthetic long position).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A 3×6 FRA (forward rate agreement) is a contract where the parties agree on:

options.A: a 3-month interest rate starting immediately, for settlement in 6 months
options.B: a 3-month interest rate starting in 3 months, settled at month 3
options.C: a 6-month rate starting in 3 months, with settlement in 9 months

questions.explanation: An m×n FRA: rate is for (n−m) months, starting in m months. A 3×6 FRA = 3-month (6−3) rate, starting in 3 months. Settlement occurs at the beginning of the loan period (month 3) on a discounted basis, not at maturity.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: In an equity swap where the fund pays the return on the S&P 500 and receives LIBOR:

options.A: the fund gains when the S&P 500 rises, as it receives positive equity returns
options.B: the fund effectively converts a long equity position to a fixed-income position
options.C: the fund retains full equity upside while hedging downside using the swap

questions.explanation: By paying the equity return and receiving LIBOR, the fund synthetic short-circuits its equity exposure and gains floating rate income — equivalent to converting a long equity position to a floating-rate (cash-like) position. If held against an existing long equity portfolio, the equity risk is neutralised.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: The CBOE VIX index measures:

options.A: the historical realised volatility of the S&P 500 over the past 30 days
options.B: the market's expectation of 30-day forward volatility derived from S&P 500 option prices
options.C: the correlation between individual S&P 500 stocks and the index

questions.explanation: The VIX (fear gauge) is computed from the implied volatilities of a wide range of S&P 500 options and represents the market's expected 30-day forward volatility. It is forward-looking, not historical. Elevated VIX signals expected turbulence.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A trader is short 100 call options, each with a delta of 0.4. To delta hedge this position, she should:

options.A: buy 40 shares of the underlying
options.B: sell 40 shares of the underlying
options.C: buy 4,000 shares of the underlying

questions.explanation: Short 100 calls × delta 0.4 = net delta of −40 (short calls have negative delta exposure to the trader). To neutralise, buy 40 shares (positive delta). Each share has delta = 1. Note: each option is on 1 share in this simplified example; in practice, standard contracts cover 100 shares each.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: An Asian option's payoff is based on:

options.A: whether the underlying price exceeds a barrier during the option's life
options.B: the average price of the underlying over the option's life
options.C: the maximum price achieved by the underlying during the option's life

questions.explanation: Asian options (average price options) use the average underlying price over a specified period as the effective price in the payoff calculation. Barrier options depend on whether a barrier is breached (answer A); lookback options use the maximum/minimum price (answer C).

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: Basis risk in a futures hedge arises when:

options.A: the futures contract is held to expiry and settled at the exact spot price
options.B: the futures price does not move perfectly in tandem with the spot price of the asset being hedged
options.C: the hedger is unable to find a liquid futures contract for the exact commodity being hedged

questions.explanation: Basis = Spot − Futures. Basis risk exists when the futures price does not perfectly track the hedged asset's spot price — due to cross-hedging, different expiry dates, or local supply/demand factors. At expiry, basis converges to zero. During the hedge, unexpected basis changes affect hedge effectiveness.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A credit-linked note (CLN) differs from a plain bond in that:

options.A: the issuer pays a lower coupon in exchange for the investor's guaranteed protection against default
options.B: the investor's principal repayment is contingent on the absence of a credit event on the reference entity
options.C: the CLN is always issued by a government entity to enhance its credit quality

questions.explanation: A CLN is a funded credit derivative. The investor buys the note (providing funding), earns a higher coupon, but bears the risk of reduced principal if a defined credit event occurs on the reference entity. It embeds a short CDS position for the investor.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: American put options may be worth more than European puts because:

options.A: American puts can be exercised early to capture intrinsic value when the underlying falls below the strike
options.B: American puts have lower time value, making immediate exercise more attractive
options.C: American and European puts are always worth the same because early exercise is never optimal

questions.explanation: Early exercise of an American put is optimal when the put is deep in-the-money — the time value of waiting is outweighed by the interest earned on the strike price received. American call options are generally not exercised early unless the stock pays dividends.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: For a deep in-the-money European call option near expiry, the time value approaches:

options.A: the intrinsic value, because time value and intrinsic value are always equal
options.B: zero, because there is little chance of the intrinsic value changing further
options.C: the stock price minus the discounted strike price

questions.explanation: Time value = Option price − Intrinsic value. For a very deep ITM option near expiry, the probability of the payoff changing significantly is minimal. Time value decays to approximately zero as expiry approaches, leaving only intrinsic value.

questions.subject: Derivatives
questions.question: A portfolio's 1-day VaR at the 99% confidence level is $1 million. This means:

options.A: the portfolio will lose more than $1 million on 99 out of 100 trading days
options.B: there is a 1% probability that the portfolio will lose more than $1 million in a single day
options.C: the expected loss given a VaR breach is exactly $1 million

questions.explanation: VaR at X% confidence = the loss that will not be exceeded with X% probability. 99% VaR of $1M means: there is a 1% chance of losing more than $1M in one day. Expected Shortfall (CVaR) measures the expected loss conditional on exceeding VaR.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A sell-side analyst reduces her rating on a company after management threatens to revoke access if she publishes a negative report. This most directly violates:

options.A: Standard V(A) – Diligence and Reasonable Basis
options.B: Standard I(B) – Independence and Objectivity
options.C: Standard II(B) – Market Manipulation

questions.explanation: Changing recommendations due to management pressure — the threat of losing access — compromises the analyst's independence and objectivity, violating Standard I(B). Maintaining independence from issuer pressure is a core requirement for sell-side analysts.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Best execution under the CFA Standards requires a manager to:

options.A: always trade with the broker offering the lowest commission
options.B: use reasonable care to maximise the value of the client portfolio, considering execution quality, cost, speed, and other relevant factors
options.C: document that every trade was executed at the national best bid and offer (NBBO)

questions.explanation: Best execution is a multi-dimensional concept: it considers price, transaction costs, speed of execution, likelihood of execution, and other factors. Lowest commission alone does not constitute best execution if other factors are compromised.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A portfolio manager issues a research note that includes a price target but omits the key assumptions and valuation model underlying the target. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(B) – Communication with Clients, by omitting material information needed to understand the recommendation
options.B: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, by presenting an unsupported conclusion
options.C: Standard III(B) – Fair Dealing, by not communicating equally to all clients

questions.explanation: Standard V(B) requires that communications include the basic characteristics of the investment, key assumptions, and limitations. A price target without supporting assumptions fails to give clients the context needed to evaluate the recommendation.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A portfolio manager negotiates a performance fee of 20% of returns above a hurdle rate with a new client. Her employer is unaware of this arrangement. She most likely violates:

options.A: Standard VI(A) – Disclosure of Conflicts, because performance fees create incentives to take excessive risk
options.B: Standard IV(B) – Additional Compensation Arrangements, because she must disclose and obtain employer consent
options.C: Standard III(C) – Suitability, because the fee structure may not be appropriate for the client

questions.explanation: Standard IV(B) requires written consent from the employer before accepting additional compensation that could create a conflict with the employer's interests. Failing to disclose the arrangement violates this standard regardless of whether the fee is fair to the client.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A candidate for the CFA exam uses an unofficial third-party summary of CFA Institute curriculum material as her primary study resource. She most likely:

options.A: violates Standard VII(A) because only official CFA materials may be used for exam preparation
options.B: does not violate any CFA Standard because the choice of study materials is the candidate's discretion
options.C: violates Standard I(C) if she presents knowledge derived from unofficial materials as her own

questions.explanation: The CFA Standards do not mandate use of official materials only — candidates may use any study aids. Standard VII(A) governs conduct during the exam and in the profession. Standard I(C) would apply if she misrepresented her qualifications, not merely the source of her preparation.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: A global macro hedge fund strategy is best described as:

options.A: taking long and short positions in equities based on fundamental mispricing between pairs of securities
options.B: making directional bets on currencies, interest rates, equities, and commodities based on macroeconomic trends
options.C: exploiting pricing discrepancies in convertible bonds relative to their underlying equity

questions.explanation: Global macro funds take large directional positions across asset classes (FX, rates, equities, commodities) based on macroeconomic analysis of policy, growth, and capital flows. Pair trades are equity long/short; convertible arb is a different strategy.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: The primary source of return in a leveraged buyout (LBO) is:

options.A: multiple expansion — buying at a low EV/EBITDA and selling at a higher multiple
options.B: a combination of financial leverage, operational improvements, and multiple expansion
options.C: dividend recapitalisations that return capital to the PE firm before exit

questions.explanation: LBO returns come from three sources: (1) financial leverage (debt amplifies equity returns), (2) operational improvements (margin expansion, revenue growth), and (3) multiple expansion (exit multiple > entry multiple). No single source dominates in all cases.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: The income approach to real estate valuation using direct capitalisation is expressed as:

options.A: Property Value = NOI / Cap Rate
options.B: Property Value = NOI × Gross Rent Multiplier
options.C: Property Value = (NOI − Debt Service) / Equity Capitalisation Rate

questions.explanation: Direct capitalisation: V = NOI / Cap Rate. The cap rate reflects required return, growth expectations, and risk. The GRM (Gross Rent Multiplier) uses gross rent — a cruder measure. The DCF method uses NOI over multiple years with a terminal value.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: The total return on a long commodity futures position consists of:

options.A: spot price return only, because futures are derivative contracts with no income component
options.B: spot price return + roll return + collateral return
options.C: convenience yield + storage cost + financing cost

questions.explanation: Commodity futures total return = Spot return (change in commodity price) + Roll return (gain/loss from rolling expiring contracts) + Collateral return (interest on margin deposited as collateral). Roll return is positive in backwardation and negative in contango.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: Infrastructure investments are typically characterised by:

options.A: high liquidity, low inflation correlation, and high short-term returns
options.B: long asset lives, stable cash flows with inflation linkage, and significant illiquidity premiums
options.C: high volatility, equity-like returns, and no regulatory oversight

questions.explanation: Infrastructure (toll roads, airports, utilities) has long-duration, regulated or contracted cash flows often explicitly or implicitly linked to inflation (CPI escalators). Illiquidity is significant — infrastructure is a long-horizon, private-market investment.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: A hedge fund charges a 2% management fee and 20% incentive fee with a high-water mark. The fund earned 15% last year (after a 10% loss the prior year). The investor's net return this year is closest to:
questions.explanation: With a HWM after a 10% loss, the fund must recover to previous peak before incentive fees apply. If NAV was 100 → fell to 90 → rose 15% to 103.5. Only gains above HWM (100) = 3.5 are subject to 20% incentive fee. Incentive = 0.20 × 3.5 = 0.70. Management = 2% × 103.5 = 2.07. Net NAV = 103.5 − 0.70 − 2.07 = 100.73. Net return to investor on start of year NAV (90): return = (100.73/90) − 1 = 11.9% ≈ 10.6% (depending on fee basis). Closest = B.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: A PE fund invested $100M in Year 0. Distributions were $60M in Year 3 and $120M in Year 5. The fund's MOIC is:
questions.explanation: MOIC (Multiple on Invested Capital) = Total Distributions / Invested Capital = (60 + 120) / 100 = 180/100 = 1.8x. MOIC ignores timing; IRR accounts for time value of money. Both are standard PE performance metrics.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: An investor who buys distressed debt at a significant discount to par is primarily seeking:

options.A: high coupon income from distressed bonds trading below par
options.B: capital appreciation from recovery above the purchase price, with potential for equity conversion via restructuring
options.C: a short position on the credit to profit from continued deterioration

questions.explanation: Distressed debt investing seeks recovery value — buying bonds at, say, 30 cents on the dollar and recovering 60–70 cents through restructuring or liquidation. Some investors gain equity in the reorganised firm. The coupon may be in default; income is secondary to recovery.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: Maximum drawdown as a risk metric for hedge funds measures:

options.A: the standard deviation of monthly returns over the fund's history
options.B: the largest peak-to-trough decline in NAV over the fund's history
options.C: the worst single-month return experienced by the fund

questions.explanation: Maximum drawdown = max(Peak NAV − Trough NAV) / Peak NAV, over the full history. It captures the worst cumulative loss an investor could have experienced. It is preferred over single-period measures for assessing tail risk in illiquid or leveraged strategies.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: Funds from Operations (FFO) is used to evaluate REITs because:

options.A: it adds back depreciation to net income, which is a non-cash charge that does not reflect property value decline
options.B: it deducts all capital expenditure to reflect the true cash cost of maintaining properties
options.C: FFO is lower than net income, providing a more conservative valuation metric

questions.explanation: Real estate properties typically appreciate over time, unlike manufactured goods. Depreciation (which reduces GAAP net income) is economically distorting for REITs. FFO = Net Income + Depreciation − Gains on property sales, providing a better measure of recurring earnings.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: A merger arbitrage strategy profits by:

options.A: shorting the acquirer and going long the target after a merger announcement
options.B: identifying undervalued companies before they are acquired and holding them for long-term appreciation
options.C: exploiting mispricings in convertible bonds relative to the issuer's equity and straight debt

questions.explanation: Merger arb: after an all-cash deal is announced, target shares trade at a discount to the announced price (deal risk premium). The arb buys the target and shorts the acquirer (in stock deals). Profit is the spread if the deal closes. Risk = deal failure.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: A commodity futures curve is in backwardation when:

options.A: futures prices are above expected future spot prices, reflecting high storage costs
options.B: futures prices are below current spot prices, often associated with high convenience yields
options.C: there is no difference between spot and futures prices due to no-arbitrage conditions

questions.explanation: Backwardation: futures price < spot price. High convenience yield (e.g., crude oil when supply is tight) pulls futures below spot. Long futures positions generate positive roll returns in backwardation as contracts are rolled to cheaper deferred contracts. Contango = futures > spot.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: The pre-money valuation of a startup is $40M and a venture capitalist invests $10M. The VC's post-investment ownership percentage is:
questions.explanation: Post-money valuation = Pre-money + Investment = $40M + $10M = $50M. VC ownership = Investment / Post-money = $10M / $50M = 20%. Pre-money valuation is the value before the investment.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: The liquidity risk premium earned by private market investors (e.g., private equity, private credit) reflects compensation for:

options.A: higher expected default rates in private markets versus public markets
options.B: the inability to quickly sell the investment at fair value, requiring a long-term capital commitment
options.C: the complexity of due diligence required for private market investments

questions.explanation: Illiquidity premium compensates investors for locking up capital for extended periods (5–10+ years) in private markets. Unlike public stocks, private investments cannot be quickly liquidated. This premium can be 1–3% per annum above comparable public market returns.

questions.subject: Alternative Investments
questions.question: The main disadvantage of a fund of hedge funds compared to direct hedge fund investment is:

options.A: reduced diversification due to concentration in a single hedge fund strategy
options.B: an additional layer of fees (2+20 at fund level plus 1+10 at FoF level) that erodes net returns
options.C: less transparency because fund of fund managers do not disclose underlying holdings

questions.explanation: Fund of funds (FoF) charges management and incentive fees on top of underlying fund fees, creating a double-fee structure that significantly reduces investor net returns. Diversification across strategies is the key benefit of FoFs, not a disadvantage.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A portfolio manager does not vote proxies because it requires time and resources. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard III(A) – Loyalty, Prudence, and Care, because proxy voting is part of the duty to clients
options.B: Standard III(B) – Fair Dealing, because not all clients receive equal representation
options.C: Standard VI(A) – Disclosure of Conflicts

questions.explanation: Standard III(A) requires managers to vote proxies in the best interest of clients. Failing to vote proxies because it is inconvenient does not fulfil the duty of loyalty and care owed to clients, as proxies are a valuable right that affects portfolio company governance.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A company selectively discloses material non-public information to a favoured analyst before the public announcement. The analyst who receives and trades on this information violates:

options.A: Standard II(A) – Material Non-Public Information
options.B: Standard I(A) – Knowledge of the Law, because Regulation FD (in the US) prohibits selective disclosure
options.C: Both Standard II(A) and potentially Standard I(A) if Reg FD applies

questions.explanation: Trading on selectively disclosed MNPI violates Standard II(A). If the analyst is operating in a US context, Regulation FD also prohibits selective disclosure, making Standard I(A) potentially relevant as well. Both standards are implicated.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A member discovers that her firm is defrauding clients by misappropriating funds. After internal escalation fails, she should:

options.A: continue working at the firm while refusing personal involvement in the fraud
options.B: dissociate from the fraud and consider reporting to regulatory authorities
options.C: disclose the fraud publicly on social media to protect investors

questions.explanation: Standard I(A) and IV(A) require that members not participate in or facilitate illegal conduct. After failed internal resolution, dissociation from the activity is mandatory. Reporting to regulators may be required by law and is consistent with Standards. Public social media disclosure is not the appropriate channel.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A quantitative analyst develops a model that generates buy/sell signals. She publishes the signals without disclosing the model's assumptions or limitations. This most likely violates:

options.A: Standard V(B) – Communication with Clients, by failing to disclose material limitations of the model
options.B: Standard V(A) – Diligence, because quantitative models require less disclosure than fundamental research
options.C: Standard I(C) – Misrepresentation, only if the model's track record is fabricated

questions.explanation: Standard V(B) requires that recommendations include sufficient information about the investment process, assumptions, and limitations to allow clients to evaluate the recommendation. Model-based signals without disclosure of key assumptions violate this standard.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: An analyst's firm has a banking relationship with a company. She is about to issue a sell rating. Her manager pressures her to change it to a hold. She should:

options.A: change the rating to avoid conflict with her employer
options.B: maintain the sell rating, support it with a reasonable basis, and document the pressure received
options.C: remove herself from coverage of the company and let another analyst issue a report

questions.explanation: Standard I(B) requires independence. The analyst must maintain her justified rating. She should document the pressure (for regulatory protection) and may escalate. If she cannot maintain independence, withdrawal from coverage may be appropriate — but changing the rating due to pressure is a direct violation.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: Relative purchasing power parity (PPP) predicts that the exchange rate between two currencies will change by:

options.A: an amount equal to the absolute price level difference between the two countries
options.B: approximately the differential in their inflation rates over time
options.C: the interest rate differential between the two countries

questions.explanation: Relative PPP: %ΔS(A/B) ≈ π_A − π_B. The country with higher inflation will see its currency depreciate by approximately that inflation differential. Absolute PPP equates price levels; interest rate differentials are covered/uncovered interest rate parity.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: The elasticities approach to current account adjustment suggests that a currency depreciation will improve the trade balance only if:

options.A: the Marshall-Lerner condition is satisfied — the sum of price elasticities of import and export demand exceeds 1
options.B: domestic income growth is slower than trading partner income growth
options.C: the country has a flexible exchange rate regime and capital controls

questions.explanation: The Marshall-Lerner condition: |ε_x| + |ε_m| > 1. If met, depreciation improves the trade balance. Initially, the J-curve effect may worsen the trade balance (quantities fixed, prices adjust), improving only later as volumes respond to price signals.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: Under a Taylor Rule framework, a central bank should raise interest rates when:

options.A: actual inflation is below the target and the output gap is negative
options.B: actual inflation exceeds the target and/or the output gap is positive (economy above potential)
options.C: the money supply growth rate exceeds the target nominal GDP growth rate

questions.explanation: Taylor Rule: i = r* + π + 0.5(π − π*) + 0.5(Y − Y*). Rates rise when inflation > target (positive inflation gap) and/or output > potential (positive output gap). Rates fall in the reverse — below-target inflation and negative output gap (recession).

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: A government increases spending by $100B and the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.8. The fiscal multiplier and the total change in GDP are:

options.A: Multiplier = 4, ΔGDP = $400B
options.B: Multiplier = 5, ΔGDP = $500B
options.C: Multiplier = 2, ΔGDP = $200B

questions.explanation: Keynesian fiscal multiplier = 1/(1−MPC) = 1/(1−0.8) = 1/0.2 = 5. ΔGDP = 5 × $100B = $500B. This assumes no crowding out, a closed economy, and spare capacity. In practice, crowding-out and Ricardian equivalence reduce the effective multiplier.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: The Solow growth model attributes long-run per capita growth to:

options.A: capital accumulation driven by high savings rates
options.B: technological progress (total factor productivity growth), as capital accumulation leads only to transitional growth
options.C: population growth, which expands the labour force and total output

questions.explanation: In the Solow model, capital accumulation faces diminishing returns. In steady state, per capita growth is zero without technological progress. Only exogenous technological change drives sustainable long-run per capita GDP growth.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: Inventory investment is considered a leading indicator of economic activity because:

options.A: businesses build inventory before expected demand increases, signalling future production plans
options.B: inventory levels reflect historical sales and lag real economic activity by 6–12 months
options.C: inventory is always at the optimal level because firms manage it perfectly

questions.explanation: Inventory build-up precedes production ramp-up and signals anticipated demand growth. Inventory liquidation (below-trend) often precedes recessions. The ISM Manufacturing PMI (inventory component) is a key leading indicator watched by economists.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: A country operating under a currency board must:

options.A: maintain full foreign exchange reserves to back every unit of domestic currency in circulation
options.B: allow the exchange rate to float freely within a narrow band managed by the central bank
options.C: fix the exchange rate unilaterally without maintaining equivalent foreign reserves

questions.explanation: A currency board commits to maintain 100% (or more) foreign reserve backing for all base money. This removes monetary policy discretion — the central bank cannot create money beyond reserve holdings. It is the strictest form of fixed exchange rate.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: Uncovered interest rate parity (UIP) implies that:

options.A: the forward exchange rate is an unbiased predictor of the future spot rate
options.B: currencies with higher interest rates are expected to depreciate by the interest rate differential
options.C: there are no arbitrage opportunities in covered forward markets

questions.explanation: UIP: E[ΔS] ≈ i_domestic − i_foreign. Higher domestic interest rates are offset by expected depreciation of the domestic currency. Covered interest parity (CIP) involves forward contracts with no currency risk; UIP involves unhedged expectations.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: Stagflation is best characterised by:

options.A: high inflation combined with rapid economic growth and low unemployment
options.B: high inflation combined with stagnant growth and high unemployment
options.C: low inflation combined with below-potential economic growth and rising unemployment

questions.explanation: Stagflation (as experienced in the 1970s) combines persistent inflation with economic stagnation and high unemployment — the worst of both worlds. It challenges traditional policy tools: rate hikes curb inflation but worsen the economic slowdown.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: A country running a persistent current account deficit is most likely:

options.A: a net lender to the rest of the world, exporting more than it imports
options.B: a net borrower from the rest of the world, financing its deficit through capital account inflows
options.C: experiencing currency appreciation due to foreign demand for domestic goods

questions.explanation: Current account deficit = excess of imports over exports. This deficit is financed by capital account surplus (foreign investment inflows — FDI, portfolio flows, borrowing). The country is a net debtor to the rest of the world.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: The Heckscher-Ohlin model predicts that a country will export goods that:

options.A: it produces most efficiently in absolute terms
options.B: use the production factor with which the country is relatively abundantly endowed
options.C: require advanced technology regardless of factor endowments

questions.explanation: H-O theory: countries export goods that use their abundant factor intensively (e.g., labour-abundant countries export labour-intensive goods). This is distinct from Ricardian comparative advantage, which focuses on relative productivity differences.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: The interest rate channel of monetary policy transmission works as follows:

options.A: Central bank changes the money supply → exchange rate adjusts → exports and imports respond
options.B: Central bank raises policy rate → borrowing costs rise → investment and consumption fall → aggregate demand declines
options.C: Central bank lowers reserve requirements → banks expand credit → asset prices rise → wealth effect boosts consumption

questions.explanation: The interest rate channel is the most direct: policy rate changes affect commercial lending rates, mortgage rates, and bond yields, which alter the cost of borrowing and the attractiveness of saving, thereby influencing investment and consumption decisions.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: Which of the following is a lagging economic indicator?

options.A: Manufacturing new orders
options.B: Average duration of unemployment
options.C: Consumer expectations

questions.explanation: Duration of unemployment is a lagging indicator — it remains elevated long after a recession ends as structural unemployment persists. New orders and consumer expectations are leading indicators. The unemployment rate itself is also typically a lagging indicator.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: Tariffs imposed by a large importing country will most likely:

options.A: reduce domestic consumer surplus and increase domestic producer surplus, with ambiguous net welfare effect
options.B: increase total economic welfare by protecting strategic domestic industries
options.C: have no effect on trade flows if trading partners impose retaliatory tariffs of equal magnitude

questions.explanation: Tariffs redistribute welfare from domestic consumers (pay higher prices) to domestic producers (protected from competition) and to the government (tariff revenue). For large importing countries, some of the cost is borne by foreign exporters (terms of trade improvement), but net welfare effect depends on specifics.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: According to the Mundell-Fleming model under perfect capital mobility with a floating exchange rate, fiscal expansion will:

options.A: stimulate GDP through increased government spending and a depreciated currency
options.B: be fully crowded out through currency appreciation that reduces net exports, leaving GDP unchanged
options.C: cause a large increase in both GDP and the current account balance

questions.explanation: Mundell-Fleming (floating FX, perfect capital mobility): fiscal expansion raises interest rates → capital inflows → currency appreciates → exports fall, imports rise → current account worsens → net exports fall, fully crowding out fiscal stimulus. Monetary policy is effective; fiscal policy is not.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: A central bank with instrument independence means it:

options.A: sets both its own inflation target and the tools used to achieve it
options.B: has the freedom to choose the tools (e.g., the policy rate) to achieve government-set objectives
options.C: is completely insulated from political pressure and answers to no government authority

questions.explanation: Instrument independence: the central bank controls the tools (policy rate, open market operations) to achieve targets set by the government. Goal independence: the central bank sets its own targets. Most central banks have instrument independence; few have full goal independence.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.topic: Purchasing Power Parity – Law of One Price
questions.question: The law of one price (LOP) states that:

options.A: identical goods traded in competitive markets will have the same price across countries when expressed in a common currency
options.B: price levels in all countries converge over time due to international trade
options.C: the purchasing power of a currency is inversely proportional to the interest rate

questions.explanation: LOP is the microeconomic foundation of PPP: without frictions (transport costs, tariffs, taxes), competitive arbitrage forces identical goods to have the same price internationally when converted to a common currency. Absolute PPP is the aggregate extension of LOP.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: An inverted yield curve (short rates above long rates) has historically been associated with:

options.A: accelerating economic growth and rising corporate profitability
options.B: an impending recession, often following a tightening cycle by the central bank
options.C: high long-term inflation expectations pulling up the long end of the curve

questions.explanation: An inverted yield curve — especially the 10Y-2Y or 10Y-3M spread — has historically preceded US recessions with considerable accuracy. It signals tight monetary policy, expected rate cuts, and slowing economic growth. High long-term inflation expectations would steepen the curve, not invert it.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.topic: Inflation – Demand Pull vs Cost Push
questions.question: Cost-push inflation is most likely caused by:

options.A: excessive money supply growth that boosts consumer spending and aggregate demand
options.B: a supply shock (e.g., oil price spike or wage increases) that raises production costs for firms
options.C: rising asset prices that increase household wealth and consumption

questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation originates on the supply side — rising input costs (energy, labour, raw materials) reduce aggregate supply, raising prices while reducing output (stagflationary dynamic). Demand-pull inflation stems from excess aggregate demand relative to potential output.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: In growth accounting, total factor productivity (TFP) growth is calculated as:

options.A: GDP growth minus population growth
options.B: GDP growth minus the weighted contributions of labour and capital growth
options.C: GDP growth divided by the capital-to-labour ratio

questions.explanation: Solow residual / TFP = Output growth − α(Capital growth) − (1−α)(Labour growth), where α is capital's share of output. TFP captures the 'residual' not explained by measured input growth — it represents technological progress, efficiency gains, and institutional quality.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: Natural monopolies are best regulated by:

options.A: breaking them up into competing firms to restore market competition
options.B: setting price equal to average total cost (ATC pricing) to allow cost recovery without economic profit
options.C: allowing the monopoly to price freely because regulation creates more distortions than monopoly pricing

questions.explanation: Natural monopolies (utilities, rail networks) have high fixed costs and declining ATC — breaking them up is inefficient. Marginal cost pricing (allocatively efficient) causes losses below ATC. ATC pricing allows cost recovery with zero economic profit — the standard regulatory solution.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: In a Nash equilibrium of an oligopoly game, each firm:

options.A: maximises joint industry profits through coordinated pricing
options.B: chooses its best strategy given the strategies of all other firms, with no incentive to deviate unilaterally
options.C: produces at the competitive equilibrium output level, driving price to marginal cost

questions.explanation: Nash equilibrium: each player's strategy is a best response to all other players' strategies. No single firm can improve its outcome by unilaterally changing strategy. In the Prisoner's Dilemma of pricing, Nash equilibrium often results in lower profits than collusion.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: A good with a price elasticity of demand of −0.3 is classified as:

options.A: elastic — consumers are highly sensitive to price changes
options.B: inelastic — consumers are relatively insensitive to price changes
options.C: unit elastic — a 1% price rise causes a 1% quantity decline

questions.explanation: Price elasticity of demand (PED) = |%ΔQ / %ΔP|. |−0.3| = 0.3 < 1 → inelastic. A 10% price rise causes only a 3% quantity decline. Elastic goods have |PED| > 1; unit elastic have |PED| = 1. Necessities and addictive goods tend to be inelastic.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: Special Drawing Rights (SDRs) issued by the IMF are best described as:

options.A: a physical gold-backed reserve currency that can be used for international settlements
options.B: an international reserve asset allocated to member countries and defined as a basket of major currencies
options.C: IMF loans extended to countries in balance of payments crisis at market interest rates

questions.explanation: SDRs are supplementary international reserve assets created by the IMF and allocated to members proportional to their quotas. They are defined by a basket (USD, EUR, CNY, JPY, GBP) and can be exchanged for freely usable currencies. They are not backed by gold and are not loans.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.question: The credit cycle typically leads the business cycle because:

options.A: credit expansion stimulates investment and consumption, which then drive economic output growth
options.B: businesses always borrow only after they observe improvements in the economy
options.C: central banks expand credit supply in response to rising GDP, making credit a lagging indicator

questions.explanation: Credit conditions precede real economic activity. Easy credit fuels investment and consumption booms (credit leads GDP up). Tightening credit (bank deleveraging, higher spreads) precedes contractions (credit leads GDP down). Financial accelerator theory formalises this pro-cyclical credit dynamic.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A CFA charterholder deletes all research notes and model files 6 months after issuing a report, citing internal storage policy. The CFA Standards require:

options.A: records to be retained for at least 7 years or as required by applicable law, whichever is longer
options.B: record retention only for client-facing documents, not internal working files
options.C: compliance with whichever is the stricter of applicable law or internal firm policy

questions.explanation: Standard V(C) recommends retaining records that support investment actions and recommendations for at least 7 years. This applies to all relevant documents — research notes, models, and communications — not just client-facing reports.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: When the interests of an employer conflict with the interests of clients, a CFA member must:

options.A: prioritise employer interests because the employment contract creates a primary legal obligation
options.B: prioritise client interests, as duties to clients supersede duties to employers under the CFA Standards
options.C: treat employer and client interests equally and seek to satisfy both parties

questions.explanation: The CFA Standards establish a clear priority: members must act in clients' best interests. Duties to employers (Standard IV) are real but subordinate to duties to clients (Standard III). An employer cannot instruct a member to harm clients.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: Publishing a research report with inflated price targets to boost trading volume in thinly traded stocks most likely violates:

options.A: Standard II(B) – Market Manipulation, by creating artificial trading activity through misleading information
options.B: Standard V(A) – Diligence, because the price targets lack a reasonable basis
options.C: Both Standard II(B) and Standard V(A)

questions.explanation: Publishing inflated targets without basis manipulates market perceptions (II(B) — information-based manipulation) and also violates the diligence and reasonable basis requirement (V(A)). Both violations are concurrent.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A manager earns a 20% performance fee and has no high-water mark. This fee structure most likely creates an incentive to:

options.A: reduce portfolio risk to protect the management fee
options.B: take excessive risk because the upside is shared with the manager but downside is not
options.C: avoid volatility to prevent underperformance that would reduce the management fee

questions.explanation: Without a high-water mark, the manager earns a new performance fee every period of positive return, even if prior losses are unrecovered. This creates a moral hazard — excessive risk-taking is incentivised because gains are shared but losses (borne by clients) aren't penalised.

questions.subject: Ethics
questions.question: A research analyst is told by her supervisor to not publish a negative report on a company because the firm hopes to win an upcoming debt issuance mandate. Publishing the report would most likely:

options.A: be required under Standard I(B) to maintain independence, even at the cost of the banking relationship
options.B: be optional — the analyst can exercise judgment about whether independence or business development takes priority
options.C: require disclosure to clients that the report was delayed due to banking considerations

questions.explanation: Standard I(B) unambiguously requires independence and objectivity. Suppressing research to preserve banking relationships violates this standard. The analyst should either publish the report or, if the firm prohibits it, should not be covering companies subject to such conflicts.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: The efficient frontier represents:

options.A: all portfolios that minimise risk for a given level of expected return or maximise return for a given level of risk
options.B: only the global minimum variance portfolio
options.C: portfolios constructed using only risk-free assets and one risky asset

questions.explanation: The efficient frontier is the set of optimal portfolios — those offering maximum expected return for each level of risk (or minimum risk for each expected return). It is the upper portion of the minimum variance frontier. Below it lie dominated portfolios.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: The Capital Market Line (CML) represents portfolios that combine:

options.A: only risky assets drawn from the efficient frontier
options.B: the risk-free asset and the market portfolio in various proportions
options.C: a riskless bond and the minimum variance portfolio

questions.explanation: The CML connects the risk-free asset with the market portfolio on the efficient frontier. All rational investors hold portfolios on the CML (combinations of the risk-free asset and the market portfolio). It represents the highest Sharpe ratio achievable.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: A security plots below the Security Market Line (SML). This indicates the security is:

options.A: overvalued — offering a lower expected return than required for its level of systematic risk
options.B: undervalued — offering a higher expected return than required for its level of systematic risk
options.C: fairly valued with zero alpha relative to the market

questions.explanation: SML plots expected return vs beta. Points below the SML = negative alpha — the security's expected return is too low for its beta. The market price is too high. Sell the security. Points above = positive alpha = undervalued; on the SML = fairly priced.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: The Fama-French three-factor model adds which factors to the CAPM's market factor?

options.A: Momentum and quality factors
options.B: Size (SMB) and value (HML) factors
options.C: Profitability and investment factors

questions.explanation: The Fama-French (1993) three-factor model: R − Rf = α + β_market × (Rm − Rf) + β_smb × SMB + β_hml × HML, where SMB = Small Minus Big (size premium) and HML = High Minus Low book-to-market (value premium). Momentum (MOM) is the fourth factor in the Carhart model.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: The Brinson-Hood-Beebower (BHB) performance attribution model decomposes active portfolio return into:

options.A: market timing, security selection, and a residual error term
options.B: allocation effect, selection effect, and interaction effect
options.C: systematic risk contribution, idiosyncratic risk contribution, and currency effect

questions.explanation: BHB attribution: Allocation effect (over/underweighting sectors vs benchmark), Selection effect (security picks within sectors vs benchmark), Interaction effect (joint effect of allocation and selection decisions). These three components sum to total active return.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR), also called Expected Shortfall (ES), is superior to VaR as a risk measure because:

options.A: CVaR is always lower than VaR, making it more conservative
options.B: CVaR captures the average loss in the tail beyond the VaR threshold, addressing tail risk that VaR ignores
options.C: CVaR is subadditive, satisfying the mathematical requirements of a coherent risk measure (unlike VaR)

questions.explanation: CVaR satisfies all four properties of a coherent risk measure: subadditivity, monotonicity, positive homogeneity, and translation invariance. Standard VaR is not subadditive (the VaR of a combined portfolio can exceed the sum of individual VaRs), violating coherence. CVaR also captures tail severity, not just the threshold.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: In a goals-based wealth management framework, a client's portfolio is typically structured as:

options.A: a single optimised portfolio targeting the maximum Sharpe ratio
options.B: multiple sub-portfolios (mental accounts), each matched to a specific goal with appropriate risk
options.C: a liability-driven portfolio that hedges all financial risks regardless of the client's goals

questions.explanation: Goals-based investing (Brunel, Chhabra) structures the portfolio around the client's life goals: a safety tier (capital preservation), a market tier (growth), and an aspirational tier (risk assets). Each sub-portfolio is calibrated to the probability of achieving its specific goal.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: Loss aversion, a key finding of prospect theory, implies that investors:

options.A: value gains and losses symmetrically relative to their wealth level
options.B: feel losses more acutely than equivalent gains, causing asymmetric risk preferences
options.C: always prefer a certain gain to a risky gain of higher expected value

questions.explanation: Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory: the pain of a loss is roughly 2–2.5× the pleasure of an equivalent gain. This causes investors to irrationally hold losing positions (to avoid realising losses) and sell winners too early.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: Strategic asset allocation (SAA) differs from tactical asset allocation (TAA) in that SAA:

options.A: is adjusted frequently to exploit short-term market mispricings
options.B: reflects the long-term target weights consistent with the investor's IPS, rebalanced periodically
options.C: uses only risk-free and market portfolio as components

questions.explanation: SAA establishes long-term target weights based on investment policy (risk tolerance, return objectives, constraints). TAA involves short-term deviations from SAA to exploit perceived market opportunities. SAA is the anchor; TAA is the active overlay.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: Calendar rebalancing differs from percentage-of-portfolio rebalancing in that calendar rebalancing:

options.A: rebalances whenever asset weights drift beyond a specified tolerance band
options.B: rebalances at fixed time intervals regardless of portfolio drift
options.C: buys assets that have underperformed and sells assets that have outperformed on a daily basis

questions.explanation: Calendar rebalancing: rebalance at predetermined intervals (monthly, quarterly). Percentage-of-portfolio (corridor) rebalancing: rebalance when any asset class drifts beyond a set band (e.g., ±5%). Corridor rebalancing is generally more cost-effective in trending markets.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: Risk budgeting in portfolio construction allocates:

options.A: capital (dollar amounts) to each asset class proportional to expected return
options.B: a share of total portfolio risk to each asset class or strategy based on its expected contribution to risk
options.C: volatility limits across individual securities to prevent any single stock from dominating returns

questions.explanation: Risk budgeting focuses on risk allocation — ensuring each strategy or asset class contributes an intended amount to total portfolio risk. A risk-parity approach (equal risk contribution) is one specific application. Capital allocation and risk allocation can differ substantially when asset class volatilities differ.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: Tax-loss harvesting as a portfolio management technique involves:

options.A: postponing capital gains realisation by holding appreciated securities longer
options.B: selling securities at a loss to offset capital gains, thereby reducing current tax liability
options.C: investing in tax-exempt securities to eliminate all investment income tax

questions.explanation: Tax-loss harvesting: realise losses in a portfolio to offset gains realised elsewhere, reducing the tax bill. The proceeds are reinvested in similar (but not substantially identical, to avoid wash sale rules) securities to maintain exposure.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: An Investment Policy Statement (IPS) should include all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: specific security selections and individual stock positions to be held
options.B: the client's return objectives and risk tolerance
options.C: investment constraints including liquidity needs, time horizon, taxes, and legal considerations

questions.explanation: An IPS defines objectives and constraints, strategic asset allocation ranges, and rebalancing guidelines — not specific individual securities. Specific security selection is delegated to the portfolio manager within the IPS framework. Including specific stocks would make the IPS a trading plan, not a policy document.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: Style analysis (returns-based) of a fund manager most likely reveals:

options.A: the specific securities held in the portfolio and their attribution to returns
options.B: the portfolio's exposures to style factors (growth, value, size, momentum) based on historical returns
options.C: whether the manager has violated investment mandates through holdings-based analysis

questions.explanation: Returns-based style analysis (Sharpe 1992) regresses a manager's returns against style factor indices to infer exposures without requiring holdings data. It reveals the effective style bets. Holdings-based analysis uses actual portfolio positions for deeper but more data-intensive insight.

questions.subject: Portfolio Management
questions.question: The Information Ratio (IR) is superior to the Sharpe Ratio for evaluating an active manager because:

options.A: the IR uses excess return over the benchmark (active return) rather than the risk-free rate, making it relevant to active management
options.B: the IR adjusts for market risk (beta) whereas the Sharpe Ratio uses total volatility
